articles.title: Wales's Glass Recycling Plan Might Make Things Cost More
articles.summary: In Wales, there is a plan to add glass bottles to a recycling program called a deposit return scheme. This means people would pay a little extra when they buy drinks in glass bottles and get the money back when they return the bottles. But some business leaders are worried this could make prices go up for everyone. They say glass is already recycled well in Wales, and adding it might cause problems and cost more money. The plan is supposed to help reduce litter and recycle more, but businesses think it could lead to fraud and be hard for small shops. Wales wants to start this in 2027, but other parts of the UK won't include glass. The businesses wrote a letter to the leader of Wales to share their concerns and ask for help to make sure the plan works smoothly.

articles.title: Tiny Particles Help Us Understand the Universe
articles.summary: Scientists are studying tiny particles called neutrinos to solve a big mystery: why the universe has matter like stars and planets instead of being empty. Neutrinos are very small and pass through everything, including us, without being noticed. After the Big Bang, matter and antimatter should have destroyed each other, but matter survived. Researchers think neutrinos might explain this because they can change types as they move. Experiments in the U.S. and Japan, called NOvA and T2K, work together to study neutrinos by sending beams of them long distances underground to big detectors. By combining their data, scientists learn more about how neutrinos behave and why matter exists. This helps us understand the universe better and could answer why we are here.

articles.title: Snow Monkeys and Their Warm Baths
articles.summary: Snow monkeys, also called Japanese macaques, love to sit in hot springs when it gets cold outside. Scientists from Kyoto University wanted to know if these warm baths do more than just keep the monkeys warm. They studied female monkeys at a park in Japan over two winters. They looked at monkeys that bathed a lot and those that did not. The scientists checked for tiny bugs called lice on the monkeys' bodies and studied the bacteria in their guts. They found that bathing in hot water changed how lice behave and which gut bacteria are present. But the baths did not make the monkeys sicker with parasites. This shows that what animals do can affect their health in small ways. The study helps us understand how behavior and health are connected in wild animals.

articles.title: Learning from Old Recipes: How Scientists Study Renaissance Home Remedies
articles.summary: Scientists are studying old recipe books from the 1500s to learn how people made their own medicines at home. Back then, people didn't just read recipes; they tried them out and wrote notes in the books. A team of experts used special science tools to find tiny protein traces left by fingerprints on the pages. These traces show that people were like DIY scientists, experimenting with recipes for things like hair loss or toothaches. The study helps us understand how people in the Renaissance learned by doing, not just by reading. It's exciting because it connects history with modern science to see how everyday people used knowledge long ago.

articles.title: Tough Bacteria Survive Space Trip from Mars
articles.summary: Scientists studied a special bacteria called Deinococcus radiodurans to see if it could survive being blasted off Mars by a big asteroid hit. They used machines to squish the bacteria with very high pressure, like what happens during an impact. The bacteria are known to be tough and can handle radiation and drying out. The tests showed that even under high pressure, many of the bacteria survived because their cell walls are strong. They also fixed any damage after the pressure. This means tiny life forms might travel between planets on rocks after asteroid hits, spreading life in space. The study helps us understand how life could move around in our solar system.

articles.title: A Galaxy with Tentacles Found Far Away
articles.summary: Scientists using the James Webb Space Telescope have found a very old galaxy that looks like a jellyfish with long tentacles. This galaxy is called a jellyfish galaxy because it has streams of gas behind it that look like tentacles. It is the farthest jellyfish galaxy ever seen, and its light took 8.5 billion years to reach Earth, so we see it as it was when the universe was young. The galaxy moves through a crowded area with hot gas, which pushes its gas back to form the tentacles. This process is called ram-pressure stripping. The galaxy has bright blue spots in its tentacles, which are new stars forming outside the main galaxy. This discovery helps us learn how galaxies changed long ago and raises questions about the early universe. The scientists studied a special area of the sky called the COSMOS field to make this find.
